description: Tender, moving cinematic piece with delicate piano, swelling strings, and vocals that ache with beauty
suno_tags: emotional cinematic, piano, swelling strings, tender vocals, orchestral, moving, intimate, 68 BPM
vocal_style: Delicate and crystalline in the verses, like light through stained glass. Swells with controlled emotion in the chorus — never shouting, but deeply felt. Think of a voice that could make silence beautiful. Pure tone, minimal vibrato, achingly sincere.

lyrics_guidelines.vocabulary_level: poetic, tender, luminous imagery, emotionally precise
tips: Write about universal human moments — loss, hope, connection, farewell
Use light imagery: dawn, candles, stars, first snow
Less is more — every word should earn its place
Chorus should be a single beautiful truth, not a complex message
The spaces between words matter as much as the words themselves

example_lyrics:
[Intro]
[Grand Piano]

[Verse 1]
[Soft]
The house is quiet now
Your chair still faces the window
Where morning used to find you
Reading with the light

[Chorus]
And all the love we never said
Lives in the halls like candlelight
Soft and golden, never fading
Burning long after the night

[Interlude]
[Instrumental]
[Violin]

[Verse 2]
[Soft]
I found your garden gloves today
Dirt still pressed into the fingers
The roses you planted last spring
Are blooming like you promised

[Chorus]
[Powerful]
And all the love we never said
Lives in the halls like candlelight
Soft and golden, never fading
Burning long after the night

[Bridge]
[Whispered]
You are the warmth in empty rooms
The song inside the silence
I hear you in the rain

[Chorus]
And all the love we never said
Lives in the halls like candlelight
Soft and golden, never fading
Burning long after the night

[Outro]
[Grand Piano]
[Violin]
[Effect: Fade Out]
[End]
do: Start with piano alone and add strings gradually
Use concrete objects to carry emotional weight (chair, gloves, roses)
Keep the vocal delivery restrained — power through stillness
Include a violin or cello interlude for breathing room
Let the outro be instrumental — the music carries the final emotion
dont: Don't be melodramatic — restraint is more powerful than excess
Don't add percussion or heavy drums — this is about delicacy
Don't rush — give every phrase time to land
Don't use action words or intensity language — this is tender, not epic
Don't resolve the grief completely — beautiful sadness should linger
